name: Alpha Lipoic Acid
historicalUse: Alpha lipoic acid is an endogenous compound discovered in the 20th century, without classical historical use as an isolated compound. However, the metabolic and mitochondrial concepts it addresses — cellular vitality, oxidative balance, and hepatic energy metabolism — are extensively addressed in classical medicine through botanical surrogates. Its modern isolation from liver tissue in 1951 represents a case where contemporary science identified the active principle behind centuries of empirical liver-supportive botanical practice.
classicalSources: Contemporary discovery — no direct classical precedent
modernEvidence: R-Alpha lipoic acid (R-ALA) is a mitochondrial coenzyme critical to the pyruvate dehydrogenase and alpha-ketoglutarate complexes. As a dietary supplement, R-ALA acts as a universal antioxidant (both fat and water soluble), regenerates vitamins C, E, and glutathione, and chelates heavy metals. Clinical applications include diabetic peripheral neuropathy (well evidenced), liver protection, and mitochondrial support. The R-form is the biologically active enantiomer.
mechanisms: Mitochondrial coenzyme — essential cofactor in TCA cycle enzyme complexes
Universal antioxidant — active in both aqueous and lipid compartments
Glutathione regeneration — reduces oxidized glutathione (GSSG) back to active GSH
AMPK activation — improves insulin sensitivity and glucose uptake
Metal chelation — binds and facilitates excretion of mercury, arsenic, cadmium
benefits: Supports antioxidant defense across all cell compartments
Supports healthy blood glucose regulation already within normal range
safetyProfile: Well tolerated. R-form preferred — more bioavailable and better tolerated than racemic (R/S) mixture. Mild hypoglycemia possible in diabetic patients on insulin or secretagogues.
contraindications: Thiamine (B1) deficiency — ALA may worsen B1 status; ensure adequate thiamine
Caution in patients with thyroid autoimmunity — theoretical iodine metabolism interaction at high doses
drugInteractions: Insulin and oral hypoglycemics — additive glucose-lowering effect; monitor blood glucose carefully
Chemotherapy (cisplatin) — theoretical protection of cancer cells at high doses; consult oncologist
Thyroid medications — monitor thyroid function if high doses used long-term
physicianNotes: R-ALA is of particular clinical value in diabetic neuropathy protocols and mitochondrial support. Ensure adequate thiamine status in patients using ALA long-term. Avoid racemic (R/S) mixtures in favor of stabilized R-form for bioavailability and tolerability.
patientGuidance: Best taken on an empty stomach or with a light meal. If you have diabetes and take insulin or blood sugar medications, monitor your glucose levels closely and inform your physician.
